title: Laryngotracheal Development: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Laryngotracheal Development
Identify key diagnostic features of Laryngotracheal Development
Apply clinical reasoning to Laryngotracheal Development management
Recognize common pitfalls in Laryngotracheal Development

sections.title: Overview of Laryngotracheal Development
sections.content:
**Laryngotracheal Development**

Laryngotracheal groove, respiratory diverticulum, tracheoesophageal septum, laryngeal anomalies

**Key Points:**
• Laryngotracheal groove appears in floor of primitive pharynx at week 4
• Respiratory diverticulum grows ventrally, separated from foregut by tracheoesophageal septum
• Incomplete septum → tracheoesophageal fistula

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Laryngotracheal groove appears in floor of primitive pharynx at week 4
• Respiratory diverticulum grows ventrally, separated from foregut by tracheoesophageal septum

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Incomplete septum → tracheoesophageal fistula
• Laryngomalacia: most common cause of stridor in infants (floppy epiglottis/arytenoids)
• Subglottic stenosis, laryngeal webs, tracheomalacia — all from developmental anomalies

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Laryngotracheal Development:**

• Incomplete septum → tracheoesophageal fistula
• Laryngomalacia: most common cause of stridor in infants (floppy epiglottis/arytenoids)
• Subglottic stenosis, laryngeal webs, tracheomalacia — all from developmental anomalies

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Laryngotracheal groove appears in floor of primitive pharynx at week 4.

coachDx.misconception: While typically self-resolving without treatment may seem plausible, it does not match the clinical scenario described.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Laryngotracheal groove appears in floor of primitive pharynx at week 4 from Typically self-resolving without treatment.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Laryngotracheal groove appears in floor of primitive pharynx at week 4.
